firms.name: Blackstone
firms.summary: World's largest PE firm. Owns Jersey Mike's, Great Wolf Lodge, Tropical Smoothie. Notorious for sale-leaseback strategies that extract real estate and saddle companies with above-market rents.

firms.name: Apollo Global
firms.summary: Owns Albertsons, Michaels ($5B LBO 2021), PetSmart (minority), and drove Claire's to bankruptcy in 2018. Also entangled in 99 Cents Only and Serta Simmons debt schemes.

firms.name: KKR
firms.summary: Pioneer of the leveraged buyout. Infamous for Toys 'R' Us where 73,000 workers lost jobs with no severance after bankruptcy in 2017. Currently loading Varsity Brands with $2.4B in junk-rated debt.

firms.name: Bain Capital
firms.summary: Infamous for Burger King, Dunkin', Domino's, Toys 'R' Us (co-investor). Pattern of debt-loading, cost-cutting, and quick exits leaving employees and communities behind.

firms.name: Leonard Green & Partners
firms.summary: Directly responsible for Joann collapse (19,000 laid off 2025), Container Store bankruptcy (Dec 2024), and Prospect Medical failure. Serially over-leverages retail and healthcare.

firms.name: Cerberus Capital
firms.summary: Owned Steward Health Care for a decade; exited 2020. Steward then filed for bankruptcy in 2024 with $9B in liabilities, closing hospitals across 8 states. 2,650+ healthcare workers lost jobs.

firms.name: Ares Management
firms.summary: Co-acquired 99 Cents Only via LBO, loaded it with $775M debt, closed 371 stores, laid off 10,800 workers. Also owns Guitar Center post-bankruptcy after converting its own debt to equity.

firms.name: Sycamore Partners
firms.summary: Retail-stripping specialist. Completed Walgreens take-private 2025. Drove Nine West to bankruptcy 2018. Known for rapidly selling assets, closing stores, and cutting workforces post-acquisition.

firms.name: JAB Holding Company
firms.summary: Secretive German family holding group that monopolized coffee and bakery. CEO admitted in 2025 to shrinking Panera portions, raising prices, switching to frozen bread. Two failed Panera IPOs.
firms.notableBk: Two failed Panera IPOs; 5%+ sales decline 2024

firms.name: BC Partners
firms.summary: Owns PetSmart via the largest LBO of 2014 ($8.7B). Controversially transferred Chewy equity outside reach of PetSmart creditors. Also co-owns Guitar Center with Ares.

firms.name: CVC Capital Partners
firms.summary: Majority owner of Petco post-IPO (still controls 60%+). Loaded Petco with $3.3B in debt. Near-bankruptcy warnings in 2023; CEO stepped down March 2024.

firms.name: BDT & MSD Partners
firms.summary: Bought majority of Whataburger in 2019, ending 69 years of family ownership. Texas Monthly 2025 documented quality concerns since PE takeover.

firms.name: Advent International
firms.summary: Bought Serta Simmons in 2012 via $3B LBO. Engaged in controversial 'uptier' debt manipulation. Filed Chapter 11 in Jan 2023 with $1.9B in liabilities. Took a 90-year-old brand to bankruptcy.

firms.name: Vista Equity Partners
firms.summary: Co-acquired Citrix for $16.5B in 2022, immediately laid off 15% of staff (~2,250 people) and discussed raising customer prices 30–40%. Also took Pluralsight private at a major loss.

firms.name: Thoma Bravo
firms.summary: Software-focused PE giant. Took Anaplan private for $10.7B in 2022, immediately laid off hundreds and offshored jobs. Glassdoor: 'cut their way to profitability at any cost.'

firms.name: Hellman & Friedman / Permira
firms.summary: Jointly acquired Zendesk for $10.2B in Nov 2022 — after Zendesk rejected a $17B offer months earlier. Price hikes and product cuts expected as PE exit targets loom.

firms.name: Silver Lake
firms.summary: Acquired Qualtrics for $12.5B in 2023. Experience management platform now focused on PE exit returns. Also holds stakes in Twitter/X and Endeavor.

firms.name: Unknown PE owner
firms.summary: Placeholder for brands whose specific PE owner is not yet tracked. The research loop should replace these with specific firm records as they are identified.

firms.name: Aurelius Group
firms.summary: German PE group. Acquired The Body Shop in 2023, drove it to bankruptcy in 2024 after closing all US stores.

firms.name: Golden Gate Capital
firms.summary: Former owner of Red Lobster. Engineered sale-leaseback that stripped real estate and pushed rents above market, directly contributing to Red Lobster's 2024 bankruptcy.

firms.name: Palantir Technologies
firms.summary: Data analytics firm that has supplied ICE with case-management and targeting software since 2013. Its FALCON system and Investigative Case Management (ICM) platform give every ICE agent access to a network of federal and private databases. In 2025, Palantir won a $30M contract to build ImmigrationOS—an AI platform for tracking, targeting, and deporting immigrants, with a prototype delivered September 2025.

firms.name: Clearview AI
firms.summary: Facial recognition company that scraped billions of images from public websites and social media without consent to build a mass surveillance database. ICE awarded Clearview AI a $9.2M contract in 2025 for facial recognition investigations. ICE began using Clearview in 2019, before completing the legally required Privacy Impact Assessment in May 2020.

firms.name: GEO Group
firms.summary: World's largest private prison operator and dominant ICE detention contractor. Operates 19+ ICE detention facilities with 26,000 beds as of 2025. Subsidiary BI Incorporated holds ICE's sole electronic monitoring contract worth $2.2B. GEO posted record $254M profit in 2025 driven by ICE detention expansion under the Trump administration. Revenue rose to $2.63B in 2025 with projections of $3B in 2026.

firms.name: CoreCivic
firms.summary: Second-largest private prison company in the U.S. and major ICE detention contractor operating at least 10 ICE facilities. Under the 2025 immigration crackdown, CoreCivic saw a 55% increase in immigration detainee contracts and signed agreements for facilities in California, Kansas, Texas, and Oklahoma totaling roughly $300M in new ICE revenue. Total 2025 revenue: $2.2B. Detainees at the California City facility reported inhumane conditions.

firms.name: Cellebrite
firms.summary: Israeli-founded digital forensics company whose Universal Forensic Extraction Devices (UFEDs) unlock phones and extract complete data images including messages, location history, encrypted app content, and photos. ICE renewed an $11M contract with Cellebrite and filed a notice of intent for a new contract worth up to $35M. DHS has contracted Cellebrite since 2008. CBP searched 47,000 devices in 2024 using similar tools.

firms.name: Thomson Reuters
firms.summary: Global media and information company whose CLEAR platform provides ICE access to license plate data, real-time incarceration data, cell phone and address records, real estate ownership, credit data, and social media information. Thomson Reuters holds six distinct contracts with ICE totaling $54.4M, including a $22.8M contract expiring 2026. Employee unions and 70% of independent shareholders have raised concerns about CLEAR's role in immigration enforcement.

firms.name: RELX Group
firms.summary: UK-headquartered information analytics company and parent of LexisNexis Risk Solutions. Its Accurint platform holds dossiers on 282 million unique U.S. identities and was awarded a $22.1M ICE contract in 2021 running through 2028. The contract gives 11,000+ ICE agents automation tools for vetting, screening, and targeting people for deportation. A 2023 lawsuit alleged Accurint enables warrantless surveillance of immigrants.

firms.name: Motorola Solutions
firms.summary: Communications technology company providing mission-critical radio, communications, and surveillance infrastructure to law enforcement. Holds a $15.6M contract with ICE to implement and maintain tactical communication infrastructure delivering secure voice and data to ICE agents, with potential extension to $28M through 2028. Also holds $391M+ in CBP contracts since 2008 for Border Patrol radio communications.

firms.name: Amazon
firms.summary: E-commerce and cloud computing giant whose Amazon Web Services (AWS) division holds 114 federal authorizations to manage DHS data in fiscal year 2025—more than any other technology company. AWS hosts ICE case management, biometric data systems, and other enforcement infrastructure. Amazon holds more federal data authorizations than any other tech company and supports the data backbone underpinning ICE enforcement operations at scale.

firms.name: Accenture
firms.summary: Global consulting and professional services firm whose federal arm, Accenture Federal Services, is the incumbent contractor on ICE's $100M+ cyber defense and intelligence support services contract. Accenture also won a $71M DHS contract to support an immigration-focused biometric and biographic identity management system. Accenture Federal Services is one of the largest IT and government services contractors enabling ICE enforcement infrastructure.

firms.name: Standard Industries
firms.summary: Private holding company headquartered in New York. Primary operations through GAF, the largest roofing manufacturer in North America. GAF's asphalt shingle plants emit polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and hazardous air pollutants. Standard Industries was ranked #1 in the PERI UMass Toxic 100 Air Polluters Index (2024 report, 2022 data), indicating the highest chronic human health risk from air toxics releases among all major U.S. corporations. Facilities have contributed to elevated cancer rates in frontline communities in Texas, Minnesota, and elsewhere. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: The Chemours Company
firms.summary: Specialty chemicals company spun off from DuPont in 2015, headquartered in Wilmington, Delaware. Chemours is one of the world's largest producers of PFAS ('forever chemicals'), including those used in Teflon-branded non-stick coatings and refrigerants. Its Washington Works facility near Parkersburg, West Virginia has a documented history of PFAS contamination that poisoned drinking water for 70,000+ people, spurring nationwide awareness of the PFAS crisis. Ranked among the top 10 parent companies in EPA's 2023 Toxics Release Inventory with 603 million pounds of waste managed. Chemours's GenX PFAS discharges have also contaminated drinking water for 500,000+ North Carolina residents. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Perdue Farms
firms.summary: Privately-held poultry and food company headquartered in Salisbury, Maryland, and one of the largest U.S. chicken producers. Perdue Farms ranked #4 among all U.S. parent companies in EPA's 2023 Toxics Release Inventory with 2,052 million pounds of chemical waste managed. Poultry processing and waste lagoons generate high volumes of ammonia and nitrogen compounds that degrade air quality in surrounding rural communities and contaminate waterways — contributing to dead zones and algal blooms in the Chesapeake Bay watershed. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Marathon Petroleum Corporation
firms.summary: One of the largest U.S. petroleum refining and marketing companies, headquartered in Findlay, Ohio, with 13 refineries and crude oil capacity of about 3 million barrels per day. Ranked #1 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2024 report, 2022 data), indicating it supplied more greenhouse-gas-emitting fossil fuel than any other U.S. company. Its refineries are major EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters, releasing benzene, volatile organic compounds, and other hazardous chemicals into neighboring communities. Sold the Speedway convenience store chain to 7-Eleven in 2021 but continues to operate Marathon-branded fuel retail. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: ExxonMobil
firms.summary: One of the world's largest publicly traded oil and gas companies, headquartered in Spring, Texas. ExxonMobil operates refineries, petrochemical plants, and over 21,000 global retail fuel stations under the Exxon, Mobil, and Esso brands. Ranked in the top 10 of PERI's Greenhouse 100 Polluters index and #5 in the Toxic 100 Water Polluters index (2022 data). Its Baytown, Texas complex is one of the largest U.S. refineries and among the top greenhouse gas emitters in the country. ExxonMobil spent decades funding climate denial while being one of the largest contributors to climate change and a major EPA TRI chemical reporter. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Valero Energy Corporation
firms.summary: The world's largest independent petroleum refiner, headquartered in San Antonio, Texas. Operates 15 refineries in the U.S., Canada, and UK with combined throughput of about 3.2 million barrels per day. Ranked #3 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2024 report, 2022 data). Valero's refinery operations are among the largest EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters, releasing volatile organic compounds, sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and other hazardous chemicals into communities near its facilities in Texas, Louisiana, and California. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Phillips 66
firms.summary: Energy manufacturing and logistics company spun off from ConocoPhillips in 2012, headquartered in Houston, Texas. Operates refineries, chemical plants, and retail fuel stations across the U.S. and internationally. Ranked #4 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2024 report, 2022 data). Phillips 66's refinery operations are significant EPA TRI reporters, contributing to air and water pollution in communities near its California, Texas, and Louisiana facilities. Owns the Phillips 66, Conoco, and 76 retail fuel brands. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Chevron Corporation
firms.summary: One of the world's largest oil and gas companies, headquartered in San Ramon, California. Operates upstream exploration, refining, and retail marketing globally under the Chevron and Texaco brands. Ranked #7 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2024 report, 2022 data). Chevron's refineries are major EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters; its Richmond, California refinery has triggered multiple air quality emergencies, including a 2012 fire that sent approximately 15,000 residents to hospital. Chevron also faced a $9.5 billion judgment (later overturned) for catastrophic oil contamination of the Ecuadorian Amazon. Acquired Texaco in 2001. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: CHS Inc.
firms.summary: The largest agricultural cooperative in the United States, headquartered in Inver Grove Heights, Minnesota. CHS operates grain handling, food manufacturing, petroleum refining, and the Cenex consumer fuel and lubricant brand. Ranked #1 among all U.S. parent companies in EPA's 2023 Toxics Release Inventory with 4,696 million pounds of chemical waste managed — the highest of any U.S. company — driven by large-scale n-hexane recycling at soybean processing facilities. CHS also operates two petroleum refineries (Laurel, MT and McPherson, KS) whose TRI releases include volatile organic compounds and other hazardous chemicals. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Koch Industries
firms.summary: The second-largest privately-held company in the United States, headquartered in Wichita, Kansas, with operations spanning oil refining (Flint Hills Resources), fertilizers (Koch Fertilizer), paper products (Georgia-Pacific), glass, electronics, and more. Ranks in the top 10 of the PERI UMass Toxic 100 Air Polluters index. Georgia-Pacific's paper mills are major EPA TRI reporters; the Green Bay, WI facility was responsible for 23% of total industrial discharge statewide in 2007-2009. The Crossett, AR mill received a $600,000 EPA Clean Air Act fine in 2018. Koch Industries has accumulated 150+ state environmental violation penalties across 20 states. Owns consumer brands Brawny, Angel Soft, Quilted Northern, Dixie, and Sparkle through Georgia-Pacific. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Duke Energy Corporation
firms.summary: One of the largest electric utility holding companies in the United States, headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina. Duke Energy serves approximately 8.4 million customers in North Carolina, South Carolina, Florida, Indiana, Ohio, and Kentucky. Ranked #2 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index (2019 EPA data) with approximately 87 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent per year — one of the highest greenhouse gas emitters of any corporation in the United States. Climate TRACE satellite-verified data corroborates Duke's coal and gas plant emissions. Despite pledges to reach net-zero by 2050, Duke continued operating multiple large coal plants through 2024 and has been sued repeatedly by environmental groups for delaying its clean energy transition.

firms.name: Southern Company
firms.summary: Major electric and gas utility holding company headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia. Ranked #3 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index (2019 EPA data) with approximately 86 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent per year. Climate TRACE satellite-verified power-sector data identifies Southern Company facilities — particularly its coal-heavy fleet in Alabama and Georgia — among the highest-emitting in the nation. Operates consumer-facing subsidiaries Georgia Power, Alabama Power, Mississippi Power, and Gulf Power, serving over 9 million customers. The Plant Vogtle nuclear expansion, while carbon-free, came in at 6B — double the original estimate — and was partly funded by ratepayer rate hikes approved by regulators.

firms.name: American Electric Power
firms.summary: One of the largest electric utilities in the United States, headquartered in Columbus, Ohio, serving approximately 5.5 million customers across 11 states including Ohio, Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, Louisiana, West Virginia, Virginia, Michigan, Indiana, and Kentucky. Ranked #5 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index (2019 EPA data) with approximately 70 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent per year — declining to 69.3 MT in 2023. Climate TRACE independently tracks AEP's coal and gas fleet as among the highest-emitting power company assets in North America. AEP operates the largest electric transmission system in the United States (39,000+ miles), giving it outsized influence over the pace of the grid's decarbonization.

firms.name: Vistra Energy
firms.summary: One of the largest competitive power companies in the United States, headquartered in Irving, Texas. Ranked #1 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index (2019 EPA data) with approximately 106.5 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent — the single largest corporate greenhouse gas emitter in the country that year. Emissions declined to approximately 86.3 MT by 2023 as older coal plants were retired, but Climate TRACE continues to identify Vistra's Texas coal fleet (including Martin Lake and Oak Grove plants) as among the highest-emitting power assets in North America. Vistra acquired Dynegy in 2018, adding more coal capacity. It also operates TXU Energy and Ambit Energy as retail electricity brands serving Texas consumers.

firms.name: NRG Energy
firms.summary: Major integrated power company headquartered in Princeton, New Jersey, serving approximately 7.4 million residential, commercial, and industrial customers across the U.S. Ranked #11 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index (2019 EPA data) with approximately 36.8 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent. Climate TRACE independently tracks NRG's large fossil fuel generation fleet in Texas and the Northeast. NRG operates Reliant Energy (retail electricity in Texas) and formerly owned Green Mountain Energy, a renewable retail provider it sold to NRG-affiliated investors. Despite marketing renewable options, NRG's core generation portfolio has remained heavily dependent on natural gas and some coal-fired capacity.

firms.name: American Airlines Group
firms.summary: The world's largest airline by scheduled passengers carried, headquartered in Fort Worth, Texas. Climate TRACE tracks aviation emissions by carrier; American Airlines' operational fleet emits approximately 45-50 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent annually, making it one of the highest-emitting airlines globally. American has made net-zero pledges for 2050 but actual emissions rose post-pandemic as travel rebounded; sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) accounted for less than 1% of fuel used in 2023. Unlike ground transportation, commercial aviation has limited near-term decarbonization pathways, and American's aggressive route expansion strategy extends its carbon commitments further into the future.

firms.name: Delta Air Lines
firms.summary: One of the largest airlines in the world by revenue, headquartered in Atlanta, Georgia. Climate TRACE satellite-verified aviation data tracks Delta's fleet as a top-tier global airline emitter. Delta reported scope 1 emissions of 35.9 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent in 2023, exceeding its own pre-pandemic 2019 baseline as travel demand rebounded — up from 30.7 MT in 2022. Delta made a high-profile  billion carbon neutrality pledge in 2020, largely based on carbon offsets, which was subsequently criticized by environmental groups and academics as greenwashing. A 2023 class-action lawsuit alleged Delta misled consumers by claiming carbon-neutral status. SAF represented less than 0.5% of fuel consumed in 2023.

firms.name: United Airlines Holdings
firms.summary: One of the three largest airlines in the United States by passengers carried, headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. Climate TRACE aviation data places United Airlines among the world's top 10 airline emitters. United reported total scope 1 emissions of approximately 36.6 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent in 2023. United has been more aggressive than peers in investing in SAF offtake agreements and emerging technology (including contrails research with Google), but SAF represented less than 1% of fuel burned in 2023 and commercial-scale availability remains a decade away. United's ongoing fleet expansion and growing international routes mean absolute emissions are rising even as carbon intensity per seat-mile improves.

firms.name: Xcel Energy
firms.summary: Major utility holding company headquartered in Minneapolis, Minnesota, serving approximately 3.7 million electric and 2.1 million natural gas customers across eight states: Colorado, Minnesota, Texas, New Mexico, South Dakota, North Dakota, Wisconsin, and Michigan. Ranked #7 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index (2019 EPA data) with approximately 47 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent — among the top 10 US corporate emitters. Climate TRACE independently tracks Xcel's coal and gas generation fleet. Xcel was one of the first utilities to pledge 100% carbon-free electricity by 2050 (later moved to 80% carbon-free by 2030), but continued operating major coal plants through 2024 and faces ongoing regulatory scrutiny over its transition timeline.

firms.name: Entergy Corporation
firms.summary: Electric utility holding company headquartered in New Orleans, Louisiana, serving approximately 3 million customers in Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas. Ranked #13 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index (2019 EPA data) with approximately 35.8 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent. Climate TRACE satellite data confirms Entergy's large gas and coal generation fleet in the Deep South as a significant regional emitter. Entergy's service territory overlaps heavily with low-income, majority-Black communities in Louisiana and Mississippi, which bear disproportionate air quality impacts from its fossil fuel generation. Entergy has faced criticism from environmental justice groups for seeking large rate increases to fund grid upgrades while delaying renewable energy investment.

firms.name: Procter & Gamble
firms.summary: World's largest consumer goods company by revenue (~$80B/year), headquartered in Cincinnati, Ohio. P&G owns dozens of household cleaning and personal care brands sold in 180+ countries. EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide grades multiple P&G cleaning products — including Tide laundry detergents and Febreze air fresheners — as D or F due to 1,4-dioxane contamination (a likely carcinogen), synthetic fragrance chemicals, optical brighteners, and other ingredients of concern. In 2019, the New York State Attorney General found elevated levels of 1,4-dioxane in Tide Original and other P&G products, prompting regulatory action. Febreze products disclose only 'fragrance' despite EWG analysis showing the category can include hormone-disrupting and allergenic compounds. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Unilever
firms.summary: Anglo-British multinational consumer goods company headquartered in London, with approximately $60B in annual revenue. Unilever sells personal care and household products in 190+ countries under brands including Dove, TRESemmé, Suave, Axe, Vaseline, and Pond's. EWG's Skin Deep database rates numerous Unilever hair care products — particularly TRESemmé — as moderate to high hazard due to formaldehyde-releasing preservatives (DMDM hydantoin), synthetic fragrances, and other ingredients of concern. TRESemmé faced class-action lawsuits in 2021-2022 alleging DMDM hydantoin caused hair loss, and multiple TRESemmé products were recalled in 2021 following FDA detection of benzene contamination. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Colgate-Palmolive
firms.summary: American consumer goods company headquartered in New York City, with approximately $20B in annual revenue. Colgate-Palmolive manufactures oral care, personal care, and household cleaning products sold globally. Its Palmolive dish soap line receives D and F grades from EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide due to methylisothiazolinone (MIT) — a potent skin sensitizer and suspected neurotoxin classified as 'high concern' by EWG — as well as synthetic fragrances and surfactants. EWG has flagged MIT-containing products as a known cause of allergic contact dermatitis, and the European Union has banned MIT from leave-on cosmetics. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: SC Johnson
firms.summary: Privately held American consumer goods company headquartered in Racine, Wisconsin, with estimated revenues of $10B+. SC Johnson manufactures household cleaning, pest control, and storage products including Windex, Scrubbing Bubbles, Raid, Off!, Pledge, Glade, and Ziploc. EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide grades multiple SC Johnson products as D or F due to quaternary ammonium compounds ('quats') in Scrubbing Bubbles bathroom cleaners — chemicals associated with reproductive toxicity, asthma, and antibiotic resistance — and synthetic fragrance in Glade products. Scrubbing Bubbles Disinfectant Bathroom Cleaner received an F from EWG specifically for quat content and chronic toxicity concerns. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Reckitt
firms.summary: British multinational consumer goods company headquartered in Slough, UK, with approximately $15B in annual revenue. Reckitt markets hygiene, health, and nutrition products globally under brands including Lysol, Finish, Woolite, Clearasil, and Durex. EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide gives multiple Lysol products D or F grades for containing quaternary ammonium compounds ('quats') — chemicals the EPA has warned may impair fertility and fetal development — as well as synthetic fragrances and other high-hazard ingredients. Lysol Disinfectant Spray received an F from EWG specifically for quat content and undisclosed fragrance chemicals. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: Edgewell Personal Care
firms.summary: American personal care company headquartered in Shelton, Connecticut, spun off from Energizer Holdings in 2015. Edgewell markets sun care, shaving, and feminine care products globally under brands including Banana Boat, Hawaiian Tropic, Schick, Wilkinson Sword, Carefree, and Stayfree. EWG's annual Sunscreen Guide has consistently ranked Banana Boat and Hawaiian Tropic products among its worst-rated sunscreens due to oxybenzone — a chemical sunscreen filter detected in human blood, urine, and breast milk at concentrations associated with hormone disruption, and confirmed to damage coral reef ecosystems at very low concentrations. Multiple Banana Boat products also contain retinyl palmitate (vitamin A), which EWG warns may accelerate skin tumor growth when used in sun-exposed skin. Tagged environment_general.

firms.name: L'Oréal
firms.summary: L'Oréal is the world's largest cosmetics company, headquartered in Clichy, France, with brands spanning mass-market and luxury beauty. Despite claims of investment in non-animal testing methods, L'Oréal continues to sell products in mainland China, where regulators require animal testing for many imported cosmetic products. PETA lists L'Oréal as a company that tests on animals as of 2024, tagging it under animals_general for continued animal testing practices in markets where it is legally required.

firms.name: The Estée Lauder Companies
firms.summary: The Estée Lauder Companies is a global luxury beauty conglomerate owning MAC, Clinique, Bobbi Brown, Tom Ford Beauty, La Mer, Aveda, and dozens of other prestige brands. The company sells products in mainland China and has confirmed it tests on animals 'where required by law.' PETA lists Estée Lauder as a company that tests on animals, placing it in the animals_general harm category for ongoing animal testing tied to market access in countries with mandatory testing requirements.

firms.name: Coty Inc.
firms.summary: Coty Inc. is one of the world's largest beauty companies, owning brands including Rimmel London, CoverGirl, Sally Hansen, OPI, and designer fragrance licenses (Hugo Boss, Gucci). Coty tests on animals where required by local regulations, including in China, and appears on PETA's companies-that-test-on-animals list. Coty has faced significant financial struggles and debt burdens following its 2016 acquisition of Procter & Gamble's beauty brands, but remains a dominant force in mass-market cosmetics. Tagged under animals_general for its continued animal testing practices.

firms.name: Church & Dwight Co.
firms.summary: Church & Dwight is an American consumer goods company best known for Arm & Hammer, OxiClean, Trojan, Vitafusion, and Waterpik. The company tests on animals for its household and personal care product lines and appears on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing. While Church & Dwight claims to minimize animal testing, it has not achieved cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Tagged under animals_general for ongoing animal testing across its product portfolio.

firms.name: Henkel AG & Co. KGaA
firms.summary: Henkel is a German multinational consumer goods company headquartered in Düsseldorf, owning hair care brand Schwarzkopf, Dial soap, Purex, Right Guard, and industrial adhesives. Henkel sells products in mainland China and tests on animals where required by law. Despite rhetoric about reducing animal testing, Henkel has not achieved cruelty-free certification and remains on PETA's companies-that-test list. Tagged under animals_general for continued animal testing practices in regulated markets.

firms.name: Beiersdorf AG
firms.summary: Beiersdorf is a German personal care company headquartered in Hamburg, most famous for its Nivea brand, as well as Eucerin, La Prairie, Hansaplast, and Coppertone. Beiersdorf sells across global markets including mainland China and tests on animals where required by local regulations. The company has not obtained cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA, and appears on PETA's companies-that-test-on-animals list. Tagged under animals_general for its animal testing practices tied to international market access.

firms.name: The Clorox Company
firms.summary: The Clorox Company is an American consumer goods manufacturer headquartered in Oakland, California, owning brands including Clorox bleach, Pine-Sol, Formula 409, Liquid-Plumr, Glad, Kingsford, Hidden Valley, and Burt's Bees. Clorox tests on animals for its cleaning product formulations and safety compliance and appears on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing. The company has not obtained cruelty-free certification. Tagged under animals_general for ongoing animal testing practices.

firms.name: Mary Kay Inc.
firms.summary: Mary Kay Inc. is a privately held American multi-level marketing cosmetics company founded in 1963 by Mary Kay Ash, headquartered in Addison, Texas. Mary Kay sells its personal care and beauty products in over 40 countries including mainland China, where regulators require animal testing for many cosmetic products. Mary Kay appears on PETA's companies-that-test-on-animals list. The company has been unable or unwilling to obtain cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Tagged under animals_general for continued animal testing tied to its China market access.

firms.name: Shiseido Company
firms.summary: Shiseido is Japan's largest cosmetics company, founded in 1872 and headquartered in Tokyo, with brands including Shiseido, NARS, bareMinerals, Clé de Peau Beauté, and Drunk Elephant. Shiseido sells extensively in mainland China, where animal testing requirements apply to many imported cosmetics. PETA lists Shiseido as a company that tests on animals. Although Shiseido has pledged to eliminate animal testing by 2027, it has not yet achieved that goal and continues to test where legally required. Tagged under animals_general for ongoing animal testing in regulated markets.

firms.name: LVMH Moët Hennessy Louis Vuitton
firms.summary: LVMH is the world's largest luxury goods conglomerate, controlled by Bernard Arnault and headquartered in Paris. Its beauty and fragrance division includes Parfums Christian Dior, Guerlain, Givenchy Parfums, Benefit Cosmetics, Make Up For Ever, and Acqua di Parma. LVMH sells luxury cosmetics and fragrances in mainland China, where animal testing requirements apply, and its beauty brands are listed by PETA as companies that test on animals. LVMH's prioritization of the lucrative Chinese market over cruelty-free standards places it in the animals_general harm category.

firms.name: Johnson & Johnson
firms.summary: Johnson & Johnson is one of the world's largest consumer health, pharmaceutical, and medical device companies. Its consumer division sells iconic personal care and skincare brands including Neutrogena, Aveeno, Clean & Clear, and Band-Aid. Despite public trust built over decades, J&J continues to conduct animal testing to meet regulatory requirements in markets like China, landing all its consumer brands on PETA's 'Does Test on Animals' list.

firms.name: GSK (GlaxoSmithKline / Haleon)
firms.summary: GSK is a global pharmaceutical and consumer healthcare company. Its consumer healthcare division was spun out as Haleon in 2022, encompassing oral care and over-the-counter brands such as Aquafresh, Sensodyne, and Panadol. Both GSK and Haleon-inherited brands remain on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing, largely to satisfy regulatory requirements in China and other markets.

firms.name: Revlon
firms.summary: Revlon is a legacy American cosmetics and fragrance company founded in 1932. The company sells mass-market beauty products under the Revlon brand and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2022 before emerging under new ownership. Despite financial restructuring, Revlon continues to be listed by PETA as a company that conducts animal testing, as it sells in markets that mandate such testing.

firms.name: Avon Products (Natura &Co)
firms.summary: Avon is a global direct-sales beauty and personal care company, now a subsidiary of Brazil-based Natura &Co following a 2020 acquisition. Avon sells cosmetics, skincare, and fragrance products in over 100 countries. Because Avon continues to sell in China—where animal testing is required for imported cosmetics—it remains on PETA's list of brands that do not meet cruelty-free standards.

firms.name: Kering
firms.summary: Kering is a French luxury conglomerate owning Gucci, Saint Laurent, Bottega Veneta, Balenciaga, and Alexander McQueen, among others. Multiple Kering-owned fragrance and beauty lines appear on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing. The company's high-fashion brands use animal-derived materials including exotic skins, fur, and down, and its cosmetics lines conduct animal testing for regulatory compliance in certain markets.

firms.name: Mars, Incorporated
firms.summary: Mars, Incorporated is a privately-held global food and pet care company known for candy brands like M&Ms, Snickers, and Skittles, and pet food brands such as Pedigree, Whiskas, and Royal Canin. In 2018 Mars acquired Blue Buffalo for $8 billion, adding a premium natural pet food brand to its portfolio. Mars conducts animal testing on its pet food formulations and is cited in cruelty-free databases for these practices.

firms.name: Clarins Group
firms.summary: Clarins is a French family-owned luxury skincare and cosmetics company founded in 1954. Known for premium botanical-based skincare products, Clarins sells globally including in mainland China, where imported cosmetics have historically required pre-market animal testing. As a result, Clarins appears on PETA's list of beauty companies that do not meet cruelty-free certification standards despite its plant-centered brand positioning.

firms.name: BIC Corporation
firms.summary: BIC Corporation is a French consumer goods company best known for disposable pens, lighters, and shavers. BIC's personal care division markets disposable razors and shaving products sold globally. The company appears on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing for its personal care and grooming product lines, as some formulations require animal safety testing for regulatory approval in certain markets.

brands.why: Vista immediately laid off 15% (~2,250 jobs). Discussed raising prices 30–40%. Banks lost $1.3B financing the deal. Employees were locked out of accounts before receiving layoff notices.
Sold to PE after rejecting a $17B offer months earlier. Now private and focused on exit returns. Customers lose public company accountability. Price hikes expected.
Thoma Bravo laid off hundreds immediately and offshored roles. Glassdoor reviews describe 'cutting 80% of the company.' PE managing for EBITDA exit, not product quality.
Taken private from SAP. All PE exits require price hikes or cuts to generate returns. Customer data inside PE-owned platforms faces monetization pressure.
Thoma Bravo took SolarWinds private in 2016 then re-IPO'd it. During PE ownership, the 2020 cyberattack hit 18,000+ organizations. PE cost-cutting on security contributed.
Blackstone closed Jan 2025. Social media already documents portion reductions. Classic early-stage PE quality extraction — typically manifests in 2–3 years.
Sold by Dobson family after 69 years. Texas Monthly 2025 documented quality concerns. PE expands rapidly on debt then cuts quality to service it.
Acquired by Blackstone June 2024. Franchise fee pressure and cost-cutting typically follow within 2–3 years of PE acquisition.
Apollo portfolio company. PE-owned chains typically cut ingredient quality to improve EBITDA for exit. Chipotle remains founder-culture focused.
PE-engineered sale-leaseback stripped real estate and raised rents above market. 100 locations closed, 36,000 jobs affected in 2024.
One of 10 PE-backed restaurant chains that went bankrupt in 2024. PE debt made adapting to post-COVID dining shifts impossible.
JAB's CEO admitted in 2025: smaller portions, higher prices, lower-quality ingredients. Switching to frozen bread — ending the fresh-baked identity. Two failed IPOs.
JAB acquired 2016, re-IPO'd 2021 but retains majority control. Rapid expansion to 14,100+ locations diluted the quality that built the brand.
JAB's first coffee monopoly acquisition. Once independent craft brand now part of a $120B extraction machine. Sourcing subordinated to margin targets.
JAB converted 88 Caribou locations to Peet's post-acquisition. Classic PE: eliminate redundancy, extract brand equity, standardize at lowest cost.
JAB paid $374M to take Einstein Noah private in 2014, then absorbed into Panera Brands 2021. Your dollar funds JAB's full extraction machine.
Part of JAB's massive fast-casual monopoly. UK staff cuts followed. Prices have risen significantly post-acquisition.
Apollo's $5B buyout loaded Michaels with debt. Preparing for PE exit via re-IPO or sale, prioritizing exit returns over investment.
Sycamore is a retail-stripping specialist. Closed hundreds of Staples locations and cut workforce repeatedly since 2017.
Sycamore portfolio company. Multiple waves of closures and staff reductions since acquisition.
Apollo drove Claire's to Chapter 11 in 2018. Emerged still burdened with debt. Textbook PE extraction of a teen-focused retailer.
Filed bankruptcy 2020 after years of PE debt-loading. Now owned by former lenders who converted debt to equity. Sweetwater is the independent gold standard.
KKR's third PE owner in a decade. $2.4B in junk-rated debt at acquisition. School athletic programs ultimately absorb higher costs.
Leonard Green drove it to bankruptcy December 2024 — same firm that bankrupted Joann and contributed to Prospect Medical's collapse.
Sycamore completed take-private 2025. Retail-stripping specialist. Mass pharmacy closures expected, reducing access in rural and low-income communities.
Apollo took ADT private for $6.9B in 2016, restructured, re-IPO'd 2018. Workers and customers bore debt costs. Apollo retains partial stake.
German PE Aurelius acquired 2023, shut all US stores by early 2024. Bankruptcy filed 2024. A brand built on ethics had its ethics immediately put up for sale.
BC Partners' $8.7B LBO was largest of 2014. Controversially transferred Chewy equity outside reach of PetSmart creditors. Apollo added minority stake 2023.
CVC loaded Petco with $3.3B in debt. Near-bankruptcy warnings 2023. CEO resigned March 2024. Still majority PE-controlled despite IPO.
Advent's $3B LBO in 2012 ended in Chapter 11 Jan 2023 with $1.9B liabilities. Debt manipulation schemes bankrupted a 90-year-old American brand.
Acquired by Serta Simmons 2018, swept into Advent's bankruptcy Jan 2023 despite being branded as a 'disruptor' mattress line.
Blackstone portfolio company. Premium pricing with ongoing margin extraction via real estate and PE fee strategies.
Apollo owned until 2020 COVID bankruptcy. PE debt burden prevented surviving the economic shock. Classic: load debt, company can't absorb a crisis, workers pay.

Palantir built ICE's case management backbone (FALCON, ICM) beginning in 2013 and in 2025 won a $30M contract to build ImmigrationOS—an AI platform for tracking, targeting, and deporting immigrants. Its software gives thousands of ICE agents access to private and federal databases containing addresses, vehicle records, and biometric data on millions of people.

Clearview AI scraped billions of photos from the internet without consent to build a mass facial recognition database. ICE awarded a $9.2M contract in 2025 to identify and target people in public. ICE began using Clearview in June 2019 before completing the legally required Privacy Impact Assessment—surveillance deployed ahead of any oversight.

GEO Group is ICE's largest private detention contractor with 26,000 beds at 19+ facilities. Its subsidiary BI Incorporated runs ICE's entire electronic monitoring program under a $2.2B sole-source contract. GEO posted record $254M profit in 2025 driven by ICE expansion. Facilities have documented violations including inhumane conditions reported by detainees.

BI Inc., a wholly owned subsidiary of GEO Group, is ICE's sole provider of electronic monitoring under a $2.2B contract. It operates ankle monitors and the SmartLINK smartphone app that surveils 187,000+ immigrants. In December 2025, ICE awarded BI Inc. a $121M skip-tracing contract to locate immigrants who miss check-ins.

CoreCivic operates ICE detention facilities including the 2,400-bed family detention center in Dilley, Texas and new facilities in California, Kansas, and Oklahoma. A 55% increase in immigration contracts in 2025 generated roughly $300M in new ICE revenue. Detainees at the California City facility reported freezing cells, sewage-filled showers, insect infestations, and denied medical care.

Cellebrite's phone-cracking devices are used by ICE to unlock seized phones and extract a complete image of all data including messages, photos, location history, and encrypted app content. ICE renewed an $11M contract and filed notice of intent for a new $35M deal. CBP searched 47,000 devices in 2024, a massive escalation from 8,500 in 2015.

Thomson Reuters' CLEAR platform gives ICE access to license plate records, incarceration data, phone and address records, real estate, credit data, and social media across six contracts totaling $54.4M. Employee unions have protested CLEAR's use for tracking and deporting immigrants with no criminal background. CLEAR data feeds directly into Palantir's ICM system used by ICE agents.

LexisNexis Risk Solutions' Accurint platform holds dossiers on 282 million U.S. residents and gives 11,000+ ICE agents automation tools for vetting, screening, and targeting people for deportation under a $22.1M contract through 2028. A 2023 lawsuit alleged Accurint enables warrantless surveillance. Advocates say ICE uses it as a backdoor around state sanctuary laws.

Motorola Solutions provides ICE with mission-critical tactical communications infrastructure under a $15.6M contract extendable to $28M through 2028. Secure radio and data networks enable ICE enforcement operations in the field. The company also holds $391M+ in Border Patrol radio contracts since 2008.

Amazon Web Services holds 114 federal authorizations to manage DHS data in FY2025—more than any other cloud provider—including ICE case management and biometric databases. AWS Government Cloud hosts the data backbone enabling ICE enforcement operations at scale. Amazon has maintained and expanded these contracts despite internal employee protests.

Accenture Federal Services is the incumbent on ICE's $100M+ cyber defense and intelligence support services contract. It also won a $71M DHS contract to manage an immigration-focused biometric and biographic identity management system. Accenture Federal is one of the largest IT contractors enabling ICE's enforcement and surveillance infrastructure.

GAF is North America's largest roofing manufacturer and a subsidiary of Standard Industries, which was ranked #1 in the PERI UMass Toxic 100 Air Polluters Index (2024 report, 2022 data) — the highest chronic human health risk from air toxic releases of any major U.S. corporation. GAF's asphalt shingle manufacturing plants emit polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and other hazardous air pollutants. Frontline communities surrounding GAF plants in Texas, Minnesota, and other states face elevated cancer risks, and residents report years of failed attempts to compel state agencies and the EPA to address the pollution.

Teflon is the world's most recognized non-stick coating brand, manufactured using PFAS ('forever chemicals') by Chemours. PFAS are highly persistent environmental pollutants that accumulate in wildlife and humans. Chemours, which inherited DuPont's PFAS business in 2015, is one of the nation's largest industrial PFAS dischargers. Its plants contaminated drinking water for 70,000+ people near Parkersburg, West Virginia and have since been found to contaminate drinking water for 500,000+ North Carolina residents with GenX PFAS compounds. Chemours ranks among the top 10 U.S. companies by total TRI waste managed (603 million pounds, 2023).

Perdue is one of America's best-known chicken brands, produced by Perdue Farms, which ranked #4 among all U.S. parent companies in the EPA's 2023 Toxics Release Inventory with 2,052 million pounds of chemical waste managed. Industrial poultry processing releases large quantities of ammonia and nitrogen into air and waterways. These emissions contribute to respiratory harm in surrounding rural communities and are a major driver of algal blooms and dead zones in the Chesapeake Bay watershed, where Perdue's operations are concentrated.

Marathon is a major U.S. gasoline retail brand operated by Marathon Petroleum, which was ranked #1 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data) — the largest U.S. supplier of greenhouse-gas-emitting fossil fuel. Marathon Petroleum's 13 refineries are significant EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters, releasing benzene, volatile organic compounds, and other hazardous chemicals into surrounding communities. As the nation's top petroleum supplier by greenhouse gas contribution, Marathon is a central driver of both local toxic pollution and global climate change.

Exxon is the primary U.S. gasoline brand of ExxonMobil, which ranks in the top 10 of the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Polluters index and #5 on the Toxic 100 Water Polluters index (2022 data). ExxonMobil is one of the largest corporate contributors to greenhouse gas emissions globally and a major EPA TRI reporter from its refinery and chemical operations. The company spent decades funding climate denial while internal research showed it understood the science of climate change, making it a central actor in delaying climate action.

Valero is a major U.S. fuel retail brand operated by Valero Energy, the world's largest independent petroleum refiner, ranked #3 in PERI's Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data). Valero's refineries are among the largest EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters in the country, releasing volatile organic compounds, sulfur dioxide, and benzene into communities near its facilities in Texas, Louisiana, and California. As one of the top U.S. fossil fuel suppliers, Valero's operations are a significant driver of both local toxic pollution and climate change.

Phillips 66 is a major U.S. fuel retail brand ranked #4 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data), making it one of the top U.S. fossil fuel suppliers by greenhouse gas contribution. Phillips 66's refinery operations — including facilities in California, Louisiana, and Texas — are significant EPA TRI reporters, releasing toxic chemicals including benzene, toluene, and sulfur dioxide into surrounding communities. The company also owns the Conoco and 76 fuel brands, giving it one of the largest consumer-facing fuel retail footprints in the U.S.

Texaco is a major U.S. gasoline retail brand now owned by Chevron Corporation, ranked #7 in the PERI UMass Greenhouse 100 Suppliers Index (2022 data). Chevron is one of the largest corporate contributors to global greenhouse gas emissions and a major EPA TRI reporter. Chevron's Richmond, California refinery has caused multiple air quality emergencies, including a 2012 fire that sent approximately 15,000 residents to the hospital. The Texaco brand is also historically associated with catastrophic Amazon oil contamination from legacy Texaco operations in Ecuador that polluted an area the size of Rhode Island.

Cenex is the consumer fuel and lubricant brand of CHS Inc., which was ranked #1 among all U.S. parent companies in the EPA's 2023 Toxics Release Inventory with 4,696 million pounds of chemical waste managed — the highest of any U.S. company. While much of this figure reflects n-hexane recycled from soybean processing, CHS also operates two petroleum refineries whose TRI releases include volatile organic compounds and other hazardous chemicals affecting communities in Montana, Kansas, and across the Midwest and Great Plains.

Brawny is a paper towel brand owned by Georgia-Pacific, a subsidiary of Koch Industries, which ranks in the PERI UMass Toxic 100 Air Polluters top 10. Georgia-Pacific's paper mills are major EPA Toxics Release Inventory reporters. The Green Bay, WI facility alone was responsible for 23% of all industrial discharge in the state (2007-2009). The Crossett, AR mill received a $600,000 EPA fine in 2018 for Clean Air Act violations; Crossett has one of the nation's highest rates of exposure to cancer-causing toxins, with Georgia-Pacific identified as the leading culprit. Koch Industries has amassed 150+ state environmental violation penalties across 20 states.

Duke Energy is one of the highest greenhouse gas emitters of any corporation in the United States, releasing approximately 87 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent per year (2019 EPA data, Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index rank #2). Climate TRACE satellite-verified emissions data corroborates its coal and gas fleet. Duke serves 8.4 million customers and continues to operate coal plants while lobbying against faster clean energy mandates in its service states.

Georgia Power is the primary consumer-facing brand of Southern Company, the third-largest corporate greenhouse gas emitter in the United States (approximately 86 million metric tons CO2e/year, UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 rank #3). Climate TRACE data identifies Southern Company's coal plants in Georgia and Alabama among the highest-emitting power assets in North America. Georgia Power serves 2.7 million customers and has been criticized for slow renewable transition timelines and seeking rate increases to fund nuclear construction overruns.

AEP Energy is the retail electricity brand of American Electric Power, the fifth-largest corporate greenhouse gas emitter in the United States (approximately 70 million metric tons CO2e/year per UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 data, declining to 69.3 MT in 2023). Climate TRACE independently verifies AEP's large fossil fuel generation fleet. AEP serves 5.5 million customers across 11 states and operates the largest high-voltage transmission system in the US, giving it outsized influence over decarbonization pace.

TXU Energy is the retail electricity brand of Vistra Energy, which was the single largest corporate greenhouse gas emitter in the United States in 2019 (approximately 106.5 million metric tons CO2e, UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 rank #1), declining to 86.3 MT in 2023. Climate TRACE satellite data identifies Vistra's Texas coal plants — including Martin Lake and Oak Grove — among the highest-emitting power assets in North America. TXU Energy is one of the largest retail electricity providers in the deregulated Texas market.

Reliant Energy is the primary retail electricity brand of NRG Energy, ranked #11 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index with approximately 36.8 million metric tons of CO2e per year. Climate TRACE tracks NRG's large fossil fuel generation fleet in Texas and the Northeast. Reliant serves hundreds of thousands of Texas households in the deregulated electricity market, marketed under various plans including green energy options — but NRG's core generation remains heavily fossil fuel-dependent.

American Airlines is one of the world's highest-emitting airlines, releasing approximately 45–50 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent annually as tracked by Climate TRACE aviation sector data. It is the world's largest airline by scheduled passengers. American made a net-zero-by-2050 pledge but sustainable aviation fuel accounted for less than 1% of fuel used in 2023 as post-pandemic demand rebounded. Aviation represents one of the hardest-to-decarbonize sectors, and American's continued fleet expansion extends its carbon commitments further into the future.

Delta Air Lines reported scope 1 greenhouse gas emissions of 35.9 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent in 2023 — exceeding its own pre-pandemic 2019 baseline — as tracked by Climate TRACE aviation data and Delta's own ESG report. Delta made a high-profile  billion carbon neutrality pledge in 2020, largely built on offset purchases, which was subsequently challenged by academics and led to a 2023 class-action greenwashing lawsuit. SAF represented less than 0.5% of Delta's fuel in 2023.

United Airlines reported total scope 1 greenhouse gas emissions of approximately 36.6 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent in 2023, placing it among the world's top 10 airline emitters as tracked by Climate TRACE. While United has been more proactive than peers in SAF offtake agreements and contrail-reduction research, SAF represented less than 1% of fuel burned in 2023. United's ongoing international expansion means absolute emissions continue rising even as carbon intensity per seat-mile improves.

Xcel Energy is one of the top 10 corporate greenhouse gas emitters in the United States (approximately 47 million metric tons CO2e/year, UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 rank #7), serving 3.7 million electric customers across eight states. Climate TRACE independently tracks Xcel's coal and gas generation fleet. While Xcel was an early utility to commit to 80% carbon-free generation by 2030, it continued operating large coal plants through 2024 and missed earlier clean energy milestones.

Entergy Corporation ranked #13 in the UMass PERI Greenhouse 100 Polluters Index with approximately 35.8 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent annually, as corroborated by Climate TRACE satellite-verified power sector data. Entergy's service territory in Arkansas, Louisiana, Mississippi, and Texas overlaps heavily with low-income, majority-Black communities who bear disproportionate air quality and climate impacts from its fossil fuel generation. Entergy has faced environmental justice criticism for delaying renewable investment while seeking rate increases.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Tide laundry products as D or F due to 1,4-dioxane — a likely human carcinogen listed by California as a Proposition 65 chemical — found as a contaminant in surfactant ingredients. The New York State Attorney General tested Tide Original in 2019 and found 1,4-dioxane levels exceeding safe thresholds, leading to new state regulations. Tide products also contain optical brighteners and undisclosed synthetic fragrance compounds that can include allergens and hormone-disrupting chemicals. EWG has campaigned for years for P&G to remove 1,4-dioxane from its cleaning formulas.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Febreze air freshener products as D or F, citing undisclosed 'fragrance' formulations that EWG testing and analysis indicates contain volatile organic compounds (VOCs), potential hormone disruptors, and respiratory sensitizers. Febreze products disclose only the generic term 'fragrance' on labels rather than individual chemical ingredients, obscuring potentially hundreds of synthetic chemicals from consumers. EWG analysis of air freshener products found chemicals including acetaldehyde (classified as a possible human carcinogen), limonene (a common sensitizer), and other compounds not disclosed on product labels.

EWG's Skin Deep database rates numerous TRESemmé shampoos and conditioners as moderate to high hazard due to DMDM hydantoin — a formaldehyde-releasing preservative linked to hair loss, scalp irritation, and allergic reactions. Multiple class-action lawsuits filed in 2021-2022 alleged TRESemmé products caused significant hair loss and scalp damage due to DMDM hydantoin content. In addition, multiple TRESemmé aerosol dry shampoo products were recalled in late 2021 after the FDA detected benzene — a known human carcinogen — in product samples. Unilever has faced sustained consumer pressure to reformulate these products.

EWG's Skin Deep database rates numerous Dove personal care products — including body washes, shampoos, and antiperspirants — as moderate concern (score 3-6 out of 10) due to synthetic fragrances, BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene, a possible endocrine disruptor), parabens in some product lines, and other ingredients of concern. Dove's 'Real Beauty' marketing positions it as a gentle, consumer-friendly brand, but EWG ingredient analysis reveals the same class of synthetic chemicals found in competitors' products, obscured by 'clean beauty' branding. The brand's aerosol dry shampoo products were also included in the 2021 benzene recall affecting multiple Unilever brands.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Palmolive dish soaps as D or F for containing methylisothiazolinone (MIT) — a preservative EWG rates as high concern for skin sensitization and suspected neurotoxicity — as well as synthetic fragrances and surfactant blends with limited safety data. MIT has been banned or severely restricted in leave-on cosmetics in the European Union due to its known capacity to cause allergic contact dermatitis. The EU Scientific Committee on Consumer Safety has also warned about MIT in rinse-off products. Despite regulatory pressure in Europe, Palmolive products sold in the United States continue to contain MIT in formulations sold as family dish soaps.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Scrubbing Bubbles Disinfectant Bathroom Cleaner as F — its worst grade — primarily for quaternary ammonium compounds ('quats') including alkyl dimethyl benzyl ammonium chloride. The EPA has registered quats as pesticides, and peer-reviewed studies have linked occupational quat exposure to asthma in healthcare workers and cleaning professionals. Animal studies show quats impair fertility and fetal development. EWG also flags the products for synthetic fragrance and chronic toxicity concerns. Despite these issues, Scrubbing Bubbles is marketed aggressively to household consumers cleaning bathrooms and kitchens.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Windex Original Glass Cleaner as D for containing 2-hexoxyethanol — a glycol ether that EWG classifies as high concern for occupational hazard and moderate concern for reproductive toxicity — as well as isopropanolamine, synthetic blue dye, and fragrance compounds not fully disclosed on the label. EWG warns that glycol ethers at high exposure can cause blood disorders and reproductive harm. While household exposure levels may be lower than occupational thresholds, EWG argues consumers deserve to know what they are spraying in enclosed spaces like kitchens and bathrooms.

EWG's Healthy Cleaning Guide rates Lysol Disinfectant Spray and multiple Lysol products as D or F for containing quaternary ammonium compounds ('quats') — EPA-registered pesticides that peer-reviewed research links to occupational asthma, reproductive harm in animal studies, and potential antibiotic resistance. EWG also flags Lysol for undisclosed fragrance formulations and other high-concern ingredients. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Lysol was ubiquitously promoted as a household disinfectant, dramatically increasing consumer and indoor air exposure to quat-containing aerosol sprays — amplifying EWG's public health concerns about chronic low-level exposure.

EWG's annual Sunscreen Guide has consistently ranked Banana Boat sunscreens among its worst-rated products, citing oxybenzone — a chemical UV filter detected in human blood, urine, and breast milk within minutes of application, and flagged by the FDA as needing further safety data before it can be classified as generally recognized as safe. EWG also found that Hawaii banned oxybenzone-containing sunscreens to protect coral reefs after studies showed it damages coral DNA at parts-per-trillion concentrations. Multiple Banana Boat products also contain retinyl palmitate (vitamin A palmitate), which EWG warns may speed development of skin lesions and tumors when applied to sun-exposed skin, based on U.S. government animal studies. In 2021, Banana Boat recalled multiple aerosol sunscreen products after FDA testing detected benzene contamination.

EWG's annual Sunscreen Guide rates multiple Hawaiian Tropic products poorly due to oxybenzone — a chemical UV filter the FDA has asked manufacturers to provide additional safety data for, and which Hawaii has banned in sunscreens due to documented coral reef damage at trace concentrations. EWG sunscreen analysts also cite inadequate UVA protection in several Hawaiian Tropic formulations relative to their advertised SPF values. High SPF numbers primarily reflect UVB protection; without adequate UVA filters, consumers may experience sunscreen-induced false confidence while incurring cumulative UVA damage. EWG recommends mineral-based sunscreens (zinc oxide, titanium dioxide) as safer alternatives.

Maybelline is owned by L'Oréal, which tests on animals to comply with regulations in mainland China and other markets. PETA lists L'Oréal as a company that tests on animals as of 2024. Maybelline is one of L'Oréal's largest mass-market cosmetics brands, and purchasing it directly funds a parent company that continues to subject animals to safety tests for market access. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Clinique is owned by The Estée Lauder Companies, which confirms it tests on animals 'where required by law,' including in mainland China. PETA lists Estée Lauder as a company that tests on animals. Despite Clinique's marketing as a dermatologist-developed, allergy-tested brand, its parent company continues to subject animals to testing to maintain access to the Chinese cosmetics market. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Rimmel London is owned by Coty Inc., which tests on animals to comply with regulations in markets including mainland China. PETA lists Coty as a company that tests on animals. Rimmel is one of the world's best-selling drugstore makeup brands, and purchasing it funds a parent company that continues to conduct or commission animal testing for regulatory compliance. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

OxiClean is owned by Church & Dwight Co., which tests on animals for its household cleaning and personal care products. PETA lists Church & Dwight as a company that tests on animals. Church & Dwight has not obtained cruelty-free certification from any major animal welfare organization. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Schwarzkopf is one of Henkel AG's flagship hair care brands, sold globally including in mainland China. Henkel tests on animals where required by local regulations and appears on PETA's companies-that-test list. Henkel has not obtained cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Purchasing Schwarzkopf products funds Henkel's ongoing animal testing practices. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Nivea is the flagship brand of Beiersdorf AG, which sells personal care products worldwide including in mainland China, where animal testing is required for many imported cosmetics. PETA lists Beiersdorf as a company that tests on animals. Beiersdorf has not received cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Purchasing Nivea products funds a parent company that continues animal testing for regulatory compliance. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Pine-Sol is owned by The Clorox Company, which tests on animals for its household cleaning products as part of safety and regulatory compliance. PETA lists Clorox as a company that tests on animals. Clorox has not achieved cruelty-free certification from any major animal welfare organization. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Mary Kay Inc. sells personal care and cosmetics products in over 40 countries including mainland China, where animal testing requirements apply to many imported cosmetics. Mary Kay appears on PETA's companies-that-test-on-animals list and has not obtained cruelty-free certification from Cruelty Free International or PETA. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Shiseido Company sells luxury and prestige skincare and cosmetics extensively in mainland China, where animal testing is required for many imported cosmetic products. PETA lists Shiseido as a company that tests on animals. Although Shiseido has pledged to eliminate animal testing, it has not yet achieved cruelty-free certification and continues to test where legally mandated. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Dior Beauty (Parfums Christian Dior) is owned by LVMH, which sells luxury cosmetics and fragrances in mainland China, where animal testing is required for many imported cosmetics. PETA lists LVMH's beauty brands as companies that test on animals. LVMH's decision to prioritize access to the Chinese luxury market means that consumers purchasing Dior Beauty products are indirectly funding animal testing. Source: https://crueltyfree.peta.org/companies-do-test/

Neutrogena, a J&J brand, appears on PETA's list of companies that do not meet cruelty-free standards. J&J sells Neutrogena in China, where imported cosmetics have required government-mandated animal testing. Until J&J restricts sales to markets that mandate animal testing, its consumer brands remain on PETA's 'Does Test on Animals' database.

Aveeno, owned by Johnson & Johnson, is marketed on natural oat-based formulas but falls short on cruelty-free claims. Because J&J distributes Aveeno in China, the brand is subject to animal-testing requirements imposed by Chinese regulators on imported cosmetics. PETA lists Aveeno under companies that do test on animals.

Aquafresh, the toothpaste brand owned by GSK/Haleon, is listed by PETA as a company that conducts animal testing. Oral care products sold in China and other regulated markets are subject to mandatory animal testing requirements, and Aquafresh continues to be sold in these markets without reformulating or withdrawing to avoid testing.

Sensodyne, the sensitive-teeth toothpaste brand under GSK/Haleon, is listed by PETA as conducting animal testing. Sensodyne is sold in mainland China where animal testing is required for imported oral care products. Despite Sensodyne's medical positioning, its parent company has not committed to removing the brand from markets that mandate animal testing.

Revlon is listed by PETA as a company that conducts animal testing on its cosmetics and beauty products. Even after its 2022 bankruptcy restructuring, Revlon continues to sell in markets such as China where animal safety testing is required by regulators for imported cosmetics. PETA maintains Revlon on its 'Does Test on Animals' list.

Avon, now owned by Natura &Co, remains on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing. Because Avon sells cosmetics and skincare in China, its products are subject to mandatory pre-market animal testing requirements for imported goods. Despite Natura &Co's sustainability commitments in its other brands, Avon has not obtained cruelty-free certification.

Balenciaga, owned by luxury conglomerate Kering, has fragrance and beauty lines that appear on PETA's list of companies conducting animal testing. Kering-owned fragrance brands use animal-derived musks and conduct regulatory animal testing in markets that require it. Balenciaga's fashion lines also routinely use exotic animal skins and other animal-derived materials.

Blue Buffalo, acquired by Mars, Incorporated in 2018 for $8 billion, is marketed as a premium natural pet food brand but falls short on cruelty-free standards. Mars conducts animal feeding trials and testing on its pet food formulations, and the company's broader track record on animal welfare includes extensive use of animal testing across its pet care portfolio. PETA lists Blue Buffalo's parent Mars as conducting animal testing.

Clarins is a luxury French skincare brand that presents itself as natural and plant-based, yet remains on PETA's 'Does Test on Animals' list. Clarins sells its products in mainland China, where until recently all imported cosmetics required pre-market animal testing by Chinese authorities. By continuing to sell in the Chinese market without meeting cruelty-free standards, Clarins does not qualify for cruelty-free certification.

BIC Razors are manufactured by BIC Corporation, which appears on PETA's list of companies that conduct animal testing. BIC's personal care products—including disposable razors and shaving gels—involve formulations that undergo animal safety testing in some markets. PETA flags BIC Corporation as not meeting cruelty-free standards for its personal care product lines.
